coredata.dc:title: Improvement of carbon dots corrosion inhibition by ionic liquid modification: Experimental and computational investigations
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Feng-Ting
Wang, Zhi-Kun
Zhou, Yi
Li, Li-Zhi
Liu, Yue-Qi
Wang, Li
Li, Chun-Ling
Zhu, Hua-Feng
Sun, Shuang-Qing
Hu, Song-Qing
coredata.dc:description: Chemical modification is effective in improving the corrosion inhibition efficiency of carbon dots (CDs) on steel. Here, the imidazolium ionic liquids (IL) modified-CDs (IL-CDs) are synthesized and proved to be mixed-type corrosion inhibitors. The IL increases the pyrrole N content and facilitates the formation of a denser inhibitor film. Molecular simulations confirm that the IL reduces the π-π interaction between CDs, endowing IL-CDs with higher water solubility and better corrosion inhibition effect than CDs. The lower orbital energy gap and larger positive electrostatic potential distribution enable the inhibition efficiency of IL-CDs of 97.11% in 1 M HCl, much superior to CDs (90.80%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corrosion inhibitor
Carbon dots
Ionic liquid
HCl solution
Molecular simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ionic liquid modified carbon dots (IL-CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and particle size distributions of CDs (a) and IL-CDs (b), FTIR (c) and UV–vis (d) spectra of CDs and IL-CDs.
XPS analysis of two kinds of CDs (a) C1s-CDs; (b) N1s-CDs; (c) C1s-IL-CDs; (d) N1s-IL-CDs.
Corrosion rate of N80 steel and inhibition efficiency of CDs (a) and IL-CDs (b) in 1 M HCl solution.
Polarization curves, Bode and Nyquist diagrams of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution with addition of different concentrations of CDs (a-c) and IL-CDs (d-f).
SEM of N80 steel in 1 M HCl (a), with 50 mg/L CDs (b), and with 50 mg/L IL-CDs (c). EDS of N80 steel in 1 M HCl (d), with 50 mg/L CDs (e), and with 50 mg/L IL-CDs (f).
SEM morphologies of N80 steel in 1 M HCl after the addition of CDs (a-d) and IL-CDs (e-h) in concentrations of 50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 150 mg/L and 200 mg/L.

XPS spectra of steel surface after corrosion tests in blank, CDs, and IL-CDs solutions. (a) is full-XPS spectrum. (b), (c), and (d) indicates the N 1 s, Fe 2p, and O 1 s spectrum of steel surface in IL-CDs solution, respectively.
Adsorption isotherms of CDs (a) and IL-CDs (b) on the surface of N80 steel at room temperature.
The LOLIPOP value of four constructed models of CDs.

Front-line orbital distribution maps and calculated E
LUMO , E
HOMO , and ΔE values of (a) CD, (b) IL-CD, (c) CD (N), (d) IL-CD (N).
The electrostatic potential distributions of (a) CD, (b) IL-CD, (c) CD (N), (d) IL-CD (N) (Isovalue=0.08).

The adsorption configuration of IL-CD (N) on metal surface (a), interaction energies between eight CD models with Fe after reaching equilibrium adsorption (b), mean square displacement (MSD) curves of Cl- along (001) direction (c), and relative concentrations curves (d) of water molecules along (001) direction. Insertion of (c) is the diffusion coefficients.
Mechanism of CDs (a, b) and IL-CDs (c, d).
Preparation processes of CDs and IL-CDs.
Corrosion parameters of N80 steel in solutions containing different concentrations of CDs and IL-CDs.
EIS parameters of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution with addition of different concentrations of CDs and IL-CDs.

head.ce:title: Improvement of carbon dots corrosion inhibition by ionic liquid modification: Experimental and computational investigations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical modification is effective in improving the corrosion inhibition efficiency of carbon dots (CDs) on steel. Here, the imidazolium ionic liquids (IL) modified-CDs (IL-CDs) are synthesized and proved to be mixed-type corrosion inhibitors. The IL increases the pyrrole N content and facilitates the formation of a denser inhibitor film. Molecular simulations confirm that the IL reduces the π-π interaction between CDs, endowing IL-CDs with higher water solubility and better corrosion inhibition effect than CDs. The lower orbital energy gap and larger positive electrostatic potential distribution enable the inhibition efficiency of IL-CDs of 97.11% in 1 M HCl, much superior to CDs (90.80%).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion caused by acid solution and other factors has brought huge economic losses and great potential danger to production safety [1–5]. How to achieve effective corrosion protection has become a critical issue modern industry must consider [6]. Corrosion inhibitor has developed into an efficient anti-corrosion technology with advantages such as low cost, strong adaptability, and simple implementation process [7,8]. The polar groups with O, N, P, S atoms and π electrons [9–13] on corrosion inhibitors can strongly adsorb on the metal surface and form a protective film against corrosion [14–16]. However, as environmental awareness continues to rise, traditional corrosion inhibitors suffer from drawbacks such as high toxicity, high cost, and serious pollution. More and more researchers are committed to the development of novel environmentally friendly, economical and efficient corrosion inhibitors [17].

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as potential green corrosion inhibitors with the advantages of excellent biocompatibility, wide source of raw materials, low cost, and simple synthesis method [18,19]. In recent years, scientists have devised various strategies to optimize the corrosion inhibition properties of ionic liquid modified CDs (IL-CDs), including but not limited to N doping [20–23], S doping and molecular modifications [24,25], and have achieved promising results. Among them, N doping has been the focus of research because of its easy implementation and high potential for performance enhancement. The corrosion inhibition was proven to originate from the possibility of lone electron pairs generated by pyridine-like N, allowing parallel adsorption of pyrrole rings on carbon steel surface to improve the coverage of ionic liquid modified CDs (IL-CDs) for more effective corrosion inhibition performance [24]. However, one of the fatal weaknesses of CDs for corrosion prevention is their tendency to agglomerate at high concentrations and their unsatisfactory long-term corrosion inhibition performance due to weak interaction [24,27]. Therefore, it is significant but still a challenge to solve the agglomeration problem of CDs in corrosion inhibition process.

In order to reduce the agglomeration of CDs and improve their adsorption and corrosion inhibition performances, many scholars pay attention to the surface chemical modification of CDs. Yang et al. synthesized ionic liquid CDs (IL-CDs) in the size of 3–5.5 nm using imidazolium ionic liquid and citric acid as green precursors, and proved that the obtained IL-CDs have an effective protective effect on Q235 steel in both 1 M HCl and 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions [26]. Similarly, Ye et al. synthesized new functionalized CDs (FCDs) using imidazole and citric acid CDs as precursors [27]. When the concentration of FCDs exceeds 100 mg/L, its inhibition efficiency to steel in 1 M HCl solution exceeds 90%. It can be evident that the functionalized CDs can increase their dispersion in the solution and their coverage area on the metal surface, greatly enhancing their adsorption strength on the steel surface and bringing excellent corrosion inhibition effect. However, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDs modified by ionic liquids has not been sufficiently demonstrated. Existing reports attribute the performance enhancement of such corrosion inhibitors to enhanced physicochemical adsorption, but there is still a lack of comprehensive explanation at the microscopic level. In especially, the weak interactions between CDs and interactions between CDs and metal surfaces are not clear.

In this study, we designed novel N-doped CDs as green corrosion inhibitor using citric acid and imidazole ionic liquids as raw precursor materials. We found that the prepared ionic liquid-modified CDs (IL-CDs) have excellent water solubility, leading to an effective reduction of the agglomeration of CDs. The inhibition efficiency of the prepared IL-CDs as corrosion inhibitors has been thoroughly explored by weight loss and electrochemical testing. The binding pattern of IL-CDs on the N80 surface was investigated by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurement. The micromorphological evolution of the N80 surface before and after the addition of CDs and IL-CDs was investigated by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM). The corrosion inhibition mechanism was elucidated by analyzing the corrosion morphology, corrosion products, and Langmuir adsorption isotherms. The effect of N doping and modification of ionic liquids on the inhibition efficiency of CDs has been fully documented by quantitative and molecular dynamics simulations. Our findings explain how to simplify the preparation process and improve the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs as green corrosion inhibitors in 1 M HCl solution.

All reagents and solvents were used as purchased without further purification. Citric acid monohydrate, 1-Bromohexane, N-(3-Aminopropyl)-imidazole, Thiourea, and Triethylamine N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride were purchased from Aladdin in analytical grade. N80 steel was purchased from Shandong Yangxin County Shengxin Technology Co., Ltd.

The CDs were synthesized as follows. 0.6 g Citric acid monohydrate and 1.2 g Thiourea were dissolved in 10 mL water within a 50 mL beaker, and then heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling down, the reaction product was dissolved in 30 mL water and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min for 3 times. The product was further purified by dialysis treatment (number average molecular weight: 500 DA) in distilled water (which was changed every 3 h) for 24 h, and then filtered through a PVDF membrane. Finally, the obtained solids (CDs) were dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 36 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ionic liquid modified carbon dots (IL-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: IL-CDs is prepared by the condensation reaction [28] between amino groups in ionic liquid and carboxyl groups in CDs. The above-prepared CDs (50 mg) were mixed with 1-ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (1 g) and dissolved in 100 mL water. The imidazole ionic liquid (1 g) was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol, and 0.5 mL triethylamine was slowly added to obtain an ionic liquid solution. Then, the mixed CDs solution was added to the ionic liquid solution and mechanically stirred for 24 h. The product was filtered with suction and purified by dialysis treatment (number average molecular weight: 500 Da), and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h. The synthesis process is illustrated in Scheme 1.

The corrosion inhibition properties of CDs and IL-CDs on N80 steel were determined by weight loss measurement at room temperature [29]. Firstly, all N80 steel samples (30 mm × 15 mm×3 mm) were polished using 800#, 1200#, and 2000# metallographic sandpapers, successively. The samples were weighed, and washed by acetone and ethanol to remove surface contaminants, and then dried in vacuum. Subsequently, these samples were added into 1 M HCl solutions containing various concentrations of CDs or IL-CDs. The immersion tests will last 24 h, after which the retrieved samples were cleaned with deionized water, placed into ethanol for ultrasonic cleaning, and finally dried in vacuum. The samples will be weighed again. The corrosion rate (mm/year) could be obtained by [30]:
(1)
r
corr
=
8.76 ×
10
4
m −
m
t
S
1
× t × ρ
where r
corr is uniform corrosion rate, mm/year; m is mass of steel sample before testing, g; m
t is mass of steel sample after testing, g; S
1 is total area of sample; ρ is density of sample, g/cm2; t is test time, h.

The structures of CDs and IL-CDs were characterized by Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, NICOLET 6700) and Ultraviolet-visible adsorption spectroscopy (UV–vis, Lambda 950). The bonding status and chemical composition of CDs and IL-CDs were characterized by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, AMICUS). The morphologies of CDs and IL-CDs were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, TF20). After weight loss tests, the morphologies of the corrosion inhibitor films were observed by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, HETACHIS-4800) and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Shimadzu SPM-9700), and the components of adsorption films were analyzed by XPS.

The electrochemical measurements were accomplished using a Gamry Reference 600 electrochemical workstation equipped with a three-electrode cell system at room temperature. The scanning potential area was 300 mv via open circuit potential (OCP), and the scanning rate was 0.1 mv/s. The scan time for OCP was 1800 s. In EIS test, the scan frequency ranged from 0.01 Hz to 100,000 Hz. Tafel curves were obtained through scanning the potential in a region of 300 mV around the OCP and the scanning rate was 0.2 mV/s. In experiments, the cuboid sample of N80 steel with a 1.0 cm2 surface area exposed to solution was the working electrode, the platinum plate was the counter electrode, and the saturated calomel was the reference electrode.

The Visualizer module in Materials Studio (Accelrys Inc.) was used to construct models of CDs with pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphite-like N (named as CD (N)), as well as models of ionic liquid-modified CD and CD (N) (named as IL-CD and IL-CD (N)). The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed based on the Dmol3 module, in which the exchange correlation energy was set to the PBE functional in GGA, and the double numerical basis was set to DNP. The molecular structures were optimized by Guassian algorithm. The NBO charges of the molecules were calculated. The Localized Orbital Locator Integrated Pi Over Plane (LOLTPOP) of the molecule was calculated by Multiwfn [31].

Molecular dynamics simulations were performed using the Forcite module. The Fe(110) surface was constructed in the size of 4.586 × 4.459 × 8.107 nm3. A solution model including 36 H3O+, 44 Cl-, and 1964 H2O representing 1 M HCl and 8 corrosion inhibitors molecule was placed on the Fe(110) surface. Protonation in HCl is considered for all carbon dots. A vacuum layer of 20 nm thickness was put on the top of the solution. All the three directions of the model were imposed periodic boundary conditions. The Fe surface was fixed, and the adsorption model was optimized by COMPASS force field [47]. Then the dynamic simulation was carried out under NVT ensemble. The simulation temperature was 298.15 K, and the temperature control method was NHL. Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution randomly generated the original motion rate of each molecule in the system, and Velocity Verlet algorithm was used to solve Newton's motion equation. The electrostatic and van der Waals interactions in the system were calculated by Ewald and Atom Based summation methods, respectively. The total simulation time was 2 ns and the time step was 1 fs. The interaction energy between the Fe surface and corrosion inhibitors was calculated by subtracting their respective energies from their total energy [48].

The TEM images of the synthesized CDs and IL-CDs in a diluted HCl solution (0.05 M) are shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b). The size of CDs ranges from 1.5 to 4.0 nm, while the size of IL-CDs ranges from 2.0 to 6.0 nm. The modification of ionic liquids makes the carbon dots size larger. In Fig. 1(a) and (b), it is found that CDs are partially agglomerated, while IL-CDs will be uniformly dispersed. Therefore, it can be inferred that IL-CDs may have better dispersibility in HCl solution than CDs. The chemical structures of CDs and IL-CDs are characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1c). The observed absorption peaks at 1184.7, 1307, 1400, 1615.7, 1643, 1717, 3000, 3420, and 1639.8 cm−1 correspond to C-OH, CN, COO-, N-H, CN, CO, C-H, O-H, and N-CO, respectively [32]. The absorption peak of N-CO at 1639.8 cm−1 indicates that the amino groups on imidazole ionic liquids have successfully reacted with the carboxyl groups on CDs [26]. Fig. 1d shows the UV–vis spectra of CDs and IL-CDs. The absorption peaks at 220 nm and 350 nm are attributed to the π-π * electronic transition of the sp
2 domain on CC and n-π * electronic transition on CN, respectively. The weak absorption peak at 450 nm is due to S doping according to the study of Song et al. [33].

The obtained CDs and IL-CDs have similar elemental compositions but different contents (Table S1). The modification of ionic liquids on CDs leads to the reduction of N contents from 15.13% to 12.37%, or more precisely, the large reduction of pyridine-like N (from 17.02% to 7.00%) and graphitic N (from 48.17% to 14.82%), and the large increase of pyrrole-like N (from 34.81% to 51.18%). It should be noted that the imidazole ionic liquid itself contains a large amount of C. Therefore, the proportion of N to the total atoms in IL-CDs is reduced, but the number of N in each molecule of IL-CDs is larger than that of CDs. The C content increases from 63.03% to 75.98%, and O content decreases from 21.63% to 11.33%, respectively. The S content of the CDs and IL-CDs is 0.21% and 0.31%, respectively, and is negligible considering its effect on the molecule adsorption.

Moreover, the XPS analyses further prove that CDs and IL-CDs have similar binding structures on the N80 surface ( Fig. 2). To understand the existing forms of C and N elements, the C 1 s peaks of CDs and IL-CDs are fitted. The observed peaks at 284.4, 285.2, 286.8, and 288.3 eV correspond to C-C/CC, C-O/CN, C-N, and CO bonds, respectively [34]. Typically, there are three peaks corresponding to C-N bonds with pyridine-like N (398.2 eV), pyrrole-like N (399.5 eV), and graphitic N (401.1 eV), respectively, that can be found in the N 1 s spectrum of CDs [35]. The modification of imidazole ionic liquid increases the content of pyrrole-like N, which may help to improve the adsorption strength of carbon dots and its corrosion inhibition performance. The results of chemical composition and bonding status are consistent with FTIR and UV–vis, and these differences between CDs and IL-CDs will contribute to different corrosion inhibition efficiencies.

The weight loss method was used to explore the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs and IL-CDs at concentrations of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L on N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution at room temperature. The effect of concentrations of CDs and IL-CDs on the corrosion rate of N80 steel and the inhibition efficiency was studied ( Fig. 3). From 10 mg/L to 50 mg/L CDs, the inhibition efficiency is increased from 77.37% to 90.80%. With further increase of CDs concentration, the inhibition efficiency starts to drop and reaches the lowest value of 47.47% (corrosion rate is 9.339 mm/a) at 200 mg/L. The high concentration of CDs would enhance the π-π interaction between molecules, which leads to the reunion of CDs and the decrease of the number of exposed active adsorption sites. This makes the CDs film not compact enough and weakens the adsorption strength, resulting in poor corrosion inhibition performance. This has been observed in our experiments that precipitation occurs when the concentration of CDs exceeds 50 mg/L, and the precipitation amount increases with further addition of CDs.

For IL-CDs, it should be noted that the inhibition efficiency ranges from 96.95% to 97.11% at concentrations from 50 mg/L to 200 mg/L, indicating that the system reaches an equilibrium inhibition performance. No precipitations appear for IL-CDs within the considered range of concentration. This is because the IL-CDs are positively charged with the incorporation of quaternized imidazole. The variation of molecular charge can be supported in Fig. S7, which indicates that the Zeta potential of CDs is − 25.7 mV, while that of IL-CDs is 57.6 mV. The electrostatic interactions between IL-CDs and water molecules become much stronger than their own π-π interactions, which greatly increase their water solubility and stability. In addition, the modification of ionic liquid will increase the active adsorption center of CDs, which greatly enhances the compactness and adsorption strength of the corrosion inhibitor film.

In order to further study the corrosion inhibition behavior of carbon dots at different concentrations, electrochemical tests were conducted. The Tafel curves of CDs and IL-CDs at concentrations of 50, 100, 150 and 200 mg/L in 1 M HCl solution were tested ( Fig. 4). The corrosion current density (i
corr ), corrosion potential (E
corr ), inhibition efficiency (IE), anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (β
a , β
c ) are obtained by extrapolating the anodic and cathodic branches in Tafel curves. The calculated parameters from Tafel curves are collected in Table 1. IE is obtained from the equation below [36,37]:
(2)
IE =
i
corr
0
−
i
corr
i
corr
0
× 100 %
where i
0
corr and i
corr are the corrosion current densities without and with corrosion inhibitor solution, respectively. The higher the β
a and β
c values, the stronger the corrosion inhibition effect of inhibitors.

With increasing concentration of CDs, the i
corr increases and the IE decreases. The maximum IE of CDs is 96.60% at 50 mg/L. After adding IL-CDs, the E
corr value of the solution is increased, while the i
corr is decreased and the corrosion is effectively inhibited. The obtained IE values of IL-CDs are between 92.55% and 94.55% with little fluctuation, indicating that the variation of corrosion inhibition efficiency has reached equilibrium. The strong self-agglomeration of CDs is speculated to lead to the reduction tendency of IE of CDs, while the modified ionic liquids on IL-CDs are supposed to effectively eliminate their self-agglomeration [2].

Compared to the blank test, the i
corr is significantly reduced after the addition of CDs or IL-CDs, indicating that the additives are effectively adsorbed on the metal surface which contributes to high blockage of metal corrosion and dissolution. To determine whether the corrosion inhibitor is cathodic or anodic oriented, previous studies assume that if the shift of E
corr after adding corrosion inhibitor is larger than − 85 mV or + 85 mV, the corrosion inhibitor is treated as cathodic or anodic oriented, respectively, and if the shift does not exceed ± 85 mV, the corrosion inhibitor is classified as mixed corrosion inhibitor [38]. Here, as the addition of CDs and IL-CDs both lead to the shift of E
corr less than ± 85 mV, they can inhibit both cathodic and anodic reactions of corrosion (i.e., mixed corrosion inhibitor).

The Nyquist and Bode diagrams of N80 steel in 1 M HCl solution containing different concentrations of CDs and IL-CDs were explored (Fig. 4). Before addition of corrosion inhibitors, the capacitive arc diameter and impedance modulus of steel are small since the contact between the steel surface and solution causes serious corrosion. After the addition of corrosion inhibitors, both values are enhanced. The Z
re value of the steel in the blank test is 18 Ω·cm2, and the impedance arc increases significantly after adding CDs. With 50 mg/L CDs, the Z
re value of the N80 steel reaches 500 Ω·cm2, indicating highly increased IE. With the decreasing concentration of CDs, the impedance arc of the system is gradually increased. Similarly, the impedance arc increases greatly after the addition of IL-CDs. The Z
re value of N80 steel is between 680 Ω·cm2 and 850 Ω·cm2. In general, the impedance arc of N80 steel becomes larger after the modification of ionic liquids on CDs. The improved corrosion performance of N80 steel can be related to the resistances of CDs and IL-CDs films, which is closely related to the film formation method of the two corrosion inhibitors. N atoms containing lone pair electrons in CDs and IL-CDs could coordinate with iron atomic vacancies on the steel surface [25]. The modification of ionic liquids on CDs introduces more N atoms to be coordinated with iron atom vacancies for enhanced adsorption strength of films. The active N atoms in ionic liquid groups play an important role in the adsorption of IL-CDs. Moreover, the self-agglomeration tendency of IL-CDs has been weakened by the existence of ionic liquid groups, which greatly enhances the bonding strength and coverage of CDs on the metal surface. Fig. S5(a) shows the Nyquist diagram of ionic liquid, and Table S3 shows its EIS parameters. The optimal inhibition efficiency of ionic liquids is 95.49%, which is worse than IL-CDs (97.07%) and better than CDs (95.12%). The ionic liquid and CDs were mixed in a mass ratio of 20:1, and the electrochemical test was carried out (Fig. S5a-c). The results show that the inhibition efficiency of the compound system is only 93.14% at 200 mg/L, which indicates that the synergistic effect of IL and CDs is not good. The enhancement of inhibition effect of IL-CDs can be attributed to the inhibition effect of imidazolium ionic liquids modified on CDs. In addition, we compared the long-term corrosion inhibition effects of CDs and IL-CDs, as shown in Fig. S6 and Table S4. The corrosion inhibition performance of IL-CDs decreased slowly with time change (i.e., the η of CDs decreases from 95.12% at 1 day to 90.76% at 3 day, 90.46% at 5 day, and 80.72% at 7 day, while the IE of IL-CDs decreases from 96.48% at 1 day to 96.63% at 3 day, 95.35% at 5 day, and 95.29% at 7 day), and it still had good corrosion inhibition performance after 7 days. However, the corrosion inhibition effect of CDs experiences a significant reduction, indicating worse long-term corrosion inhibition effect than that of IL-CDs.

The electrochemical impedance spectrogram (EIS) parameters from Fig. 4 are collected in Table 2, and the fitting circuit diagram is shown in Fig. S1. Where, R
s , R
f , R
ct , C
f , and C
dl are solution resistance, steel resistance, charge transfer resistance, film capacitance, and double-layer capacitance, respectively. η was calculated by the polarization resistance in the inhibited solution (R
p
= R
f
+ R
ct ) and blank solution (R
p,0
= R
ct,0 ): [5,39,40]:
(3)
η =
R
p
−
R
p , 0
p
× 100 %

The R
s values of N80 steel are similar for all experiments. R
f increases gradually with the increase of IE, indicating the adsorption film is getting denser. At the same time, R
ct and IE have the same variation tendency because corrosion inhibitors with small dielectric constant can replace water molecules with large dielectric constant, thereby reducing the exposed area of steel in solution. Compared with CDs, IL-CDs have larger R
ct , which indicates that the corrosion inhibition film formed by IL-CDs is denser and has larger binding strength with the metal surface.

To explore the difference between IL-CDs and CDs in corrosion resistance of the metal surface, N80 carbon steels were immersed in 1 M HCl solutions (without inhibitors, with 50 mg/L CDs, and with 50 mg/L IL-CDs, respectively) for 24 h at room temperature. The surface corrosion morphologies and element contents were studied by SEM ( Fig. 5a-c), EDS (Fig. 5d-f), and AFM (Fig. 5h-j). The obtained elemental compositions and contents of steel surfaces are collected in Table S2. The surface of N80 steel with the addition of CDs is smoother than the blank sample without the addition of inhibitors. In the HCl solution added with IL-CDs, the scratches on N80 steel are still visible, indicating that the steel is very slightly corroded. IL-CDs have a denser corrosion inhibitor film and better hydrophilicity, as shown in Fig. S2. It can be seen from the EDS spectrum that when no corrosion inhibitors are added, the surface of the sample is dominated by Fe and O. The O content on the steel surface is decreased from 44.42% to 30.14% and 18.00%, while the Fe content is increased from 37.39% to 55.44% and 63.53%, respectively. At the same time, S element appears on the surface of the sample, indicating the adsorption of CDs and IL-CDs on the steel surface.

The AFM images display the 3D corrosion morphologies of N80 steel in different solutions. Without the addition of CDs or IL-CDs, the N80 steel surface has very dense corrosion pits but a small Ra value of 127.2 nm, indicating that the steel is uniformly corroded in 1 M HCl. With the addition of CDs and IL-CDs, the corrosion pits on the N80 steel surface become fewer and fewer, but the Ra value increases to 156.2 nm and 178.7 nm, respectively. This indicates that the steel corrosion has been inhibited due to the protection of added inhibitors, while the increasing Ra value may be due to the formation of local defective inhibitor films.

In order to further clarify the adsorption states of corrosion inhibitor films on the steel surface, the SEM images of the steel surface in solutions with different concentrations of CDs and IL-CDs were obtained ( Fig. 6). For CDs, the formed protection films on the steel surface are dense at 50 mg/L and 100 mg/L, while the protection films become highly defective at 150 mg/L and 200 mg/L. This behavior intuitively exhibits the self-aggregation of CDs that leads to the reduction of inhibition efficiency. For IL-CDs, the smoothest surface can be obtained at 100 mg/L, and small protrusions appear on the surface as the concentration increases. Compared with CDs films, the inhibitor films formed by the IL-CDs are more compact, which explains why IL-CDs have better inhibition efficiency.

XPS analysis was used to determine the adsorption type of CDs and IL-CDs on the steel surface after the corrosion test ( Fig. 7). After immersion in the blank solution without any inhibitors, C, O and Fe elements are detected on the steel surface. When IL-CDs are added to the solution, a new N element can be observed in the XPS full spectrum (Fig. 7a), indicating the successful adsorption of IL-CDs on the steel surface. By Gaussian fitting, the N 1 s spectrum can be fitted to three peaks located at 398.8 eV, 399.6 eV, and 400.3 eV (Fig. 7b). The first peak is attributed to unprotonated N atoms (C-N) on IL-CDs. The second peak is related to the N on IL-CDs coordinated to the steel surface (N-Fe). The third peak corresponds to protonated N on IL-CDs. The existence state of N on the steel surface indicates that the adsorption type of IL-CDs is the combination of both chemical and physical adsorptions.

In order to explore the adsorption mode and adsorption strength of CDs and IL-CDs, adsorption isotherms were plotted ( Fig. 8). The adsorption strength and mode of CDs and IL-CDs are reflected by their adsorption isotherms [41]:
(4)
θ
1 − θ
=
K
ads
C
where θ, K
ads , and C represent the surface coverage, adsorption equilibrium constant, and inhibitor concentration, respectively. In general, a larger K
ads value represents stronger adsorption of inhibitors [42]. Very good linear correlations can be obtained when the concentration of CDs is lower than 100 mg/L (R
2 =0.998), and the concentration of IL-CDs ranges from 50 mg/L to 200 mg/L (R
2 =1). In addition, the adsorption types of CDs and IL-CDs are determined by calculating the adsorption free energy (ΔG
0
ads ) [43]:
(5)
∆
G
ads
0
= − RT ln
1000
K
ads
where R and T are the molar gas constant 8.314 J/(mol⋅K) and the absolute temperature (298.15 K), 1000 represents the water molecules concentration, g/L, respectively. If the value of ΔG
0
ads is greater than − 20 kJ/mol or smaller than − 40 kJ/mol, the adsorption type will be treated as physical or chemical adsorption, respectively [44]. If the value of ΔG
0
ads lies between − 20 kJ/mol and − 40 kJ/mol, the adsorption type will be treated as the combination of both physical and chemical adsorptions [45]. From our calculations, the K
ads and ΔG
ads value of CDs are 373.65 L/g and − 31.79 kJ/mol, while that of IL-CDs are 1885.16 L/g and − 35.80 kJ/mol, respectively. Thus, the adsorption types of the two inhibitors on the N80 surface are both the combination of physical and chemical adsorptions. The relatively larger K
ads and smaller ΔG
ads values of IL-CDs than CDs indicate that the modification of ionic liquid groups improves the chemical adsorption strength of CDs.

In order to explore the effect of N doping and ionic liquid modification on the structure of CDs, molecular models of pure CD, CD (N) (i.e., CD doped with pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N), IL-CD, and IL-CD (N) (i.e., IL-CD doped with pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N) were constructed, respectively.

In terms of the self-agglomerations of CDs, LOLIPOP (Localized Orbital Locator Integrated Pi Over Plane) index was calculated to evaluate the π-stacking abilities of the four models ( Fig. 9). The larger the LOLIPOP index is, the weaker the π-stacking ability will be. It can be seen that the LOLIPOP value of CD is 0.955, and becomes 0.986 after N doping of CD (N). After ionic liquid modification, the LOLIPOP value of IL-CD is 1.150 and is increased to 3.421 after N doping of IL-CD (N). These observations indicate that N doping can weaken the π stacking effect of CDs to a certain extent, and the ionic liquid modification can greatly strengthen this effect, which makes IL-CD more stable in static solution and less prone to precipitation (i.e., denser corrosion inhibitor film).

In order to further analyze the effect of ionic liquid modification on the activity of CDs, the electrostatic potential distribution was calculated ( Fig. 11). The value of the isosurface (Isovalue) is set to 0.08. Compared with CD, the electrostatic potential of CD (N) containing pyrrole-like N and graphitic-like N becomes more positive. After ionic liquid modification, the overall electrostatic potential of the two molecules becomes more positive, especially near the imidazole rings. This can be attributed to the net positive charges in IL-CD and IL-CD (N) that raising the electrostatic potential of the entire molecule, while for CD and CD (N) the local distribution of electrostatic potential can be positive or negative to maintain the electrical neutrality of the entire molecule. This variation indicates that the electronic activity, i.e., molecular activity, of CDs has been greatly enhanced due to the modification of ionic liquids. Again, our simulations demonstrate why IL-CDs are more likely to adsorb on the steel surface to form stronger and denser corrosion inhibitor films. The Fukui indexes of the four molecules are shown in Fig. S3. The active sites of CDs are mainly concentrated on the carbon ring, N, and O. These positions play an important role in the adsorption of CDs.

The adsorption configurations of CD, CD(N), IL-CD, IL-CD(N) models on the metal surface are shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. 12a. Fig. 12b shows the adsorption energy of carbon dots on Fe surface after equilibrium adsorption. IL-CD (N) with the most N atoms has the largest adsorption energy of 79.33 eV, which is greater than IL-CD (78.66 eV), CD(N) (69.77 eV), and CD (61.49 eV). This phenomenon is related to its adsorption, as shown in Fig. S4e-i. IL-CD (N) has a larger coverage area and is closer to the surface, while CD is farther away from the surface and has the smallest coverage area. This also leads to the different ability of carbon dots to limit Cl- and water molecule displacement. The mean square displacement (MSD) curves and diffusion coefficients of Cl- (Fig. 12c) and water molecules (Fig. S4c) were calculated from the equilibrium solution model with corrosion inhibitor molecules adsorbed on the Fe surface. The order of the diffusion coefficient of Cl- in the four models is IL-CD (N) (0.47 ×10−8 m2·s−1) < IL-CD (0.49 ×10−8 m2·s−1) < CD (N) (0.50 ×10−8 m2·s−1) < CD (0.52 ×10−8 m2·s−1), corresponding to the order of IL-CD (N) > IL-CD > CD (N) > CD on inhibiting the displacement ability of Cl-. This shows that the carbon dots modified by ionic liquids can effectively restrain the displacement ability of Cl-, which may be attributed to its stronger adsorption. The radial distribution function and relative concentration curves of water and Cl- show that the number of water molecules on the metal surface is decreased after the adsorption of IL-CD and IL-CD (N) (Fig. 12d, Fig. S4a and d), which is also the reason why the performances of IL-CD and IL-CD (N) are better than that of CD and CD (N). In addition, as shown in Fig. S4b, IL-CD and IL-CD (N) have larger coverage areas, resulting in the number reduction of Cl- distributed on the metal surface, and contributing to better protection effects of them.

The adsorption patterns of CDs and IL-CDs are summarized and depicted in Fig. 13. The mechanism of action of the CDs is to form an adsorption film on the steel surface. Chemisorption is attributed to the lone pair of electrons of N atoms, which readily fill unoccupied 3d orbitals other than iron atoms, and then form coordinate bonds between the heteroatoms and the steel substrate. However, increasing the concentration of CDs will cause their own agglomeration effect, resulting in the formation of a non-uniform film on the steel surface. The positive charge of the ionic liquid leads to electrostatic repulsion between the molecules of IL-CDs, which alleviates the agglomeration and exposes more active adsorption sites. Meanwhile, since the ionic liquid itself contains more active sites, IL-CDs have smaller π-π interactions, stronger reactivity, and more adsorption sites. This is why IL-CDs can form a denser adsorption film, resulting in better anticorrosion performance. The adsorption of CDs mainly depends on the chemical bonds formed by N atoms and the retrodonation bonds of the carbon ring structure. IL-CDs have more N atoms, which can form ligand bonds with metal surface in the HCl solution. Besides, the characteristics of Fe make it easier to combine with the metal surface by electrostatic attraction.

In conclusion, the CDs inhibitor modified by imidazolium ionic liquids (IL-CDs) has been successfully prepared, which exhibits remarkable inhibition efficiency of 97.11%, surpassing the unmodified CDs of 90.80%, as confirmed by the weight loss experiments. This favorable anti-corrosion property was also substantiated by a series of electrochemical measurements. We noticed that the inhibition ability of the pristine CDs can be largely reduced at relatively higher concentrations (from 100 mg/L to 200 mg/L) due to their unexpected self-agglomeration. The unexpected self-agglomeration behavior can be avoided in IL-CDs systems due to the enhanced water solubility. To reveal the origin of the higher inhibition performance and this bewildering phenomenon, we collected the adsorption isotherm curves of CDs and IL-CDs and found that the adsorption capability of IL-CDs was dramatically enhanced by ionic liquids owing to the introduction of quaternized imidazole rings in the molecules. Moreover, DFT calculations illuminated that the superior performance of IL-CDs was chalked up to the lower energy gap, more positive electrostatic potential distribution and weaker π-π interaction, which can be ascribed to the regulation of imidazolium ionic liquids. This work may give a deeper insight into the design and mechanism exploration of CDs as advanced corrosion inhibitors.

Feng-ting Li and Zhi-Kun Wang conducted the experiment and wrote the paper; Feng-Ting L and Yi Zhou performed the characterization measurements. Li-Zhi Li, Yue-Qi Liu, Li Wang, Hua-Feng Zhu and Shuang-Qing Sun carried out some data analysis and proposed valuable suggestions. Chun-Ling Li and Song-Qing Hu designed and supervised this study. All authors contributed to the general discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.
